english: 1. the consonant which is formed in the lowest and hindmost part of the organs of speech, being produced by the opening of the glottis, like the Greek spiritus lenis, the Hebrew Aleph and the Arabian Elif. (In our modern languages the opening of the glottis is not regarded as a consonant, nor expressed by a particular letter or sign.) Combined with the Tibetan vowel-signs, ཨ, ཨི, ཨུ, ཨེ, ཨོ, it is pronounced 'a, 'i, 'u, 'e, 'o (cf. འ). It is also called skye-ba-méd-pai yi-ge (skye ba med pa'i yi ge), probably because all speaking depends on and is rendered possible only by a previous opening of the glottis; hence this letter is a symbol of the deity, of the čós-sku (chos sku) that was before every thing else. Spyan-ras-zigs (spyan ras gzigs), therefore, addresses a celestial Buddha with 'a: 'a skye-méd rnam-dág čós-kyi dbyíṅs ('a skye med rnam dag chos kyi dbyings). - 2. num.: 30.

1. in Ld. and Kh. the col. demonstr. pron, for de (de) that (q.v.); *’á-ne* (*'a ne*) from thence, there, ’á-ru ('a ru) there, thither, that way. - 2. Lh., pronounced very short and sharp, well? what is the matter? yes! here!
Cs.: 'Ssk.: अ, a mystical exclamation'.
acc. to Huc II, 160 = ’a-jò ('a jo) Kh.

Lt., Ssk.: untimely.
an alpine plant, in Lh. Arenaria Roylea.
an exclamation expressive of contempt and detestation, opp. to ’á-la-la ('a la la); acc. to Cs. a-k´ág (a khag) is also adj. = mi-sdug-pa (mi sdug pa).
W. also ’a-k´ú ('a khu), col. for k´u-bo (khu bo) 1. father's brother, uncle Mil., C., W. - 2. husband, consort W.

caressing word used by mothers soothing their babies, prob. without any particular signification Thgy.

interj. expressive of pain from cold, hence ’a-cú-zer-ba ('a cu zer ba) n. of one of the cold hells.
Bal. ’a-śé ('a she), col. for c´e-źe (che zhe) 1. an elder sister of a female person. - 2. W. wife, mistress, madam, used as address and otherwise.

C., W., jo-jò (jo jo) C. (v. jò-bo (jo bo)) 1. an elder brother of a male person. - 2. Sir, Mr., gentleman, lord, used in addressing and otherwise; also like our: friend! ho there! hollo! I say! ’a-jò lág ('a jo lags) C. the old Squire, = ga-gá (ga ga) Ld.
Ssk., p´ul-du-byuṅ-ba (phul du byung ba) Tib., n. of a celebrated Pandit of Bengal, who lived for many years in Tibet, and died there in the eleventh century of our era.
